topic: sound-and-complete orbit-balance characterization for exact conformal cluster-effect prediction under finite matched-block randomization: for each known block permutation group and assignment support, prove equality of calibration- and target-selector mass on every member orbit iff selected scores are universally exchangeable over all bounded invariant block laws and equivariant learners; give a finite selector-feasibility LP and block-level rank-inversion algorithm, quantitative bounded undercoverage from every orbit gap, zero-coverage disjoint-orbit and paired-mate-leakage witnesses, the S2 matched-pair reduction, and the Shamba Maisha eight-pair facility consumer

open_problems.open_problem: For a finite matched-block assignment group, is equality of calibration-selector and target-selector mass on every group orbit necessary and sufficient for universal exchangeability of selected conformal scores over all bounded invariant block laws and equivariant learners?

source_refs.claim: The paper establishes finite-sample conformal prediction intervals for cluster- and individual-level treatment effects with arbitrary working outcome models, but does not give a necessity characterization for general assignment supports and selectors.
source_refs.author: Bingkai Wang, Fan Li, Mengxin Yu

source_refs.claim: The paper bases exact permutation inference on finite-group invariance and orbit arguments, while warning that transformation sets lacking the needed group structure can invalidate a test.
source_refs.author: Jesse Hemerik, Jelle Goeman

source_refs.claim: A randomized modification yields valid permutation p-values for arbitrary, possibly nonuniform distributions on arbitrary subsets of permutations; the paper treats tests, not universal conformal-score exchangeability.
source_refs.author: Aaditya Ramdas, Rina Foygel Barber, Emmanuel J. Candès, Ryan J. Tibshirani

source_refs.excerpt: A single random pair T cannot simultaneously have the distinct Q_v marginals required for every v
condition on the single actual ratio w, then formulate robust validity uniformly over laws

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing cluster-conformal theory supplies sufficient exchangeability constructions, and finite-group testing supplies orbit-based exactness arguments, but neither characterizes the exact relationship between two distinct selectors on a restricted assignment support. The prior CausalSmith review exposed the coupling obstacle directly: one target draw cannot realize incompatible candidate marginals. Sufficiency may follow from orbitwise balance, whereas necessity requires constructing, for each individual orbit-mass discrepancy, a bounded invariant block law and equivariant learner whose selected score ranks detect that discrepancy.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An if-and-only-if theorem equating universal selected-score exchangeability with orbitwise calibration-to-target selector-mass balance, together with a bounded converse construction for every violated orbit.

writing_exemplar.why: It gives the closest motivation arc from few-cluster asymptotic fragility to finite-sample, model-robust causal-effect prediction.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: cluster trials often have too few clusters for reliable asymptotics -> conformal ranks can give finite-sample treatment-effect prediction -> exactness under general blocked assignment requires knowing precisely when calibration and target scores share the same randomization law

method_exemplars.technique: Condition on a finite-group orbit and prove rank validity from invariance; adapt the orbit decomposition to the two selector-induced measures and prove the converse with separating score functions.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired characterization is an orbitwise equivalence under a finite transformation group, the same algebraic object underlying their exactness proof.

method_exemplars.technique: Invert conformal score ranks for causal contrasts while allowing arbitrary fitted outcome models, and retain finite-sample coverage through score symmetry.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It already constructs the target cluster-effect intervals to which an orbit-balance certificate would be attached.

open_problems.open_problem: Given an arbitrary finite matched-block assignment support and target selector, can feasibility of exact conformal calibration be decided by a finite nonnegative-weight linear program and converted into an exact block-level rank-inversion algorithm?

source_refs.claim: Permutation tests can remain valid with arbitrary distributions over arbitrary permutation subsets after a specific randomized correction, showing that uniform full-group enumeration is not necessary.
source_refs.author: Aaditya Ramdas, Rina Foygel Barber, Emmanuel J. Candès, Ryan J. Tibshirani

source_refs.claim: The paper constructs strategically chosen subgroups that preserve exact group-invariance testing and reduce computation, but its design problem is subgroup choice rather than calibration-to-target selector matching.
source_refs.author: Nick W. Koning, Jesse Hemerik

source_refs.claim: The paper develops efficient algorithms for finite-sample cluster- and individual-effect conformal intervals, but not a feasibility certificate for arbitrary blocked supports and selector constraints.
source_refs.author: Bingkai Wang, Fan Li, Mengxin Yu

open_problems.why_unsolved: Arbitrary permutation distributions can be made valid for testing, and well-chosen subgroups can reduce computation, but conformal causal prediction must match a target selector's mass rather than merely generate a valid null reference distribution. Restricted assignment supports may omit entire target-relevant orbits, turning existence into a constrained mass-transport problem; the cited work gives neither a finite feasibility certificate nor a link from a feasible selector weighting to causal-effect rank inversion.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite LP whose feasibility is equivalent to orbitwise calibration of the target selector, a dual infeasibility certificate, and a terminating block-level algorithm that inverts the resulting weighted ranks into an exact treatment-effect prediction set.

writing_exemplar.why: Its framing directly questions the presumed need for uniform sampling from a full permutation group and replaces it with an arbitrary-support construction.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: full permutation groups are computationally prohibitive -> exact testing can use nonuniform distributions on arbitrary subsets -> exact conformal causal prediction still needs a certificate that the available calibration support can reproduce the target selector

method_exemplars.technique: Exploit subgroup structure to preserve finite exactness and explicitly construct economical transformation sets; recast the construction as orbit-incidence constraints with a dual separation witness.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The proposed LP similarly selects or weights a restricted transformation support while preserving an exact finite-group property.

method_exemplars.technique: Compute causal-effect conformal intervals through efficient score inversion under arbitrary working learners and prove finite-sample coverage.
method_exemplars.why_closest: A feasible selector-weight solution must ultimately be converted into the same kind of rank-inverted cluster-effect prediction interval.

open_problems.open_problem: What is the sharp worst-case undercoverage of a finite-randomization conformal effect interval as a function of orbitwise calibration-to-target selector imbalance, including whether every positive orbit gap admits an attaining bounded invariant law and zero-coverage witness?

source_refs.claim: The paper bounds conformal coverage loss under departures from exchangeability and nonsymmetric learning, using weighted quantiles and randomized symmetrization, but does not derive sharp finite-orbit lower bounds.
source_refs.author: Rina Foygel Barber, Emmanuel J. Candès, Aaditya Ramdas, Ryan J. Tibshirani

source_refs.claim: The paper shows that selection breaks calibration-test exchangeability and restores exact selection-conditional coverage using reference sets for permutation-invariant selection rules.
source_refs.author: Ying Jin, Zhimei Ren
source_refs.venue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society Series B

source_refs.claim: The paper quantifies leakage when a transformation collection differs from the exact null reference structure, but studies test power and size rather than conformal undercoverage from selector imbalance.
source_refs.author: Nick W. Koning, Jesse Hemerik

open_problems.why_unsolved: Robust conformal results primarily give sufficient upper controls based on global nonexchangeability measures, while selective conformal work repairs validity by rebuilding a reference set. Neither pins the minimax coverage deficit to the signed mass gaps of individual finite-group orbits. Sharpness is nontrivial because a single bounded invariant outcome law and equivariant learner must simultaneously realize the selector discrepancy and force the adverse rank ordering.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp finite-sample inequality mapping orbitwise selector gaps to worst-case coverage loss, plus bounded invariant constructions attaining the bound and explicit disjoint-orbit regimes with zero coverage.

writing_exemplar.why: It frames coverage failure quantitatively as a consequence of broken exchangeability and develops robustness guarantees rather than treating validity as binary.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: ordinary conformal validity rests on exchangeability and learner symmetry -> deployment or selection breaks those symmetries -> finite blocked experiments need a sharp orbit-level accounting of exactly how much coverage is lost

method_exemplars.technique: Couple swapped score laws and bound rank-coverage loss by a discrepancy from exchangeability; adapt the proof to selector measures restricted to finite orbits.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The desired theorem is likewise a quantitative finite-sample coverage guarantee under controlled nonexchangeability.

method_exemplars.technique: Construct a data-dependent reference set by replaying selection with each calibration point treated as focal, then prove conditional exchangeability within that set.
method_exemplars.why_closest: Orbitwise target-calibration mismatch is a structured selection failure, and replaying focal selection suggests both upper bounds and adverse witnesses.

method_exemplars.technique: Condition on transformation orbits and use exact finite rank counting, including tie handling.
method_exemplars.why_closest: An attaining undercoverage witness can be constructed orbit by orbit only after reducing coverage to a finite rank-counting problem.

open_problems.open_problem: For pair-matched cluster trials, what exact two-element-group reduction characterizes valid conformal prediction of a target cluster effect, and precisely when does using the target cluster's randomized mate as calibration cause finite-sample coverage failure?

source_refs.claim: The paper derives asymptotically exact ATE inference for matched-pair cluster trials and finite-sample validity of within-pair permutation tests only for specific null hypotheses.
source_refs.author: Yuehao Bai, Jizhou Liu, Azeem M. Shaikh, Max Tabord-Meehan

source_refs.claim: The paper provides finite-sample conformal treatment-effect prediction for cluster trials, but does not isolate the two-element matched-pair assignment group or a mate-leakage necessity result.
source_refs.author: Bingkai Wang, Fan Li, Mengxin Yu

source_refs.claim: Shamba Maisha randomized 16 Kenyan health facilities as eight matched pairs, providing a small-support application where exact pair-respecting prediction is substantively relevant.
source_refs.author: Craig R. Cohen et al.

source_refs.claim: The paper shows that common analyses of pair-matched cluster randomization can have invalid standard errors and develops design-based estimators that respect the pairing.
source_refs.author: Kosuke Imai, Gary King, Clayton Nall

open_problems.why_unsolved: Matched-pair randomization has the simple local group consisting of identity and within-pair swap, but causal-effect prediction introduces a target selector absent from ordinary randomization tests. The target's mate is assignment-dependent and can cease to be a valid calibration analogue once the target is selected. Existing matched-pair theory addresses ATE estimators or sharp-null tests, while existing cluster conformal theory does not state the exact selector condition or an explicit mate-leakage counterexample.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A complete reduction to the two-element swap group giving necessary and sufficient selector conditions, an exact paired rank-inversion rule, and an explicit bounded potential-outcome table where mate leakage yields zero coverage.

writing_exemplar.why: It clearly introduces pair-matched cluster assignment, separates superpopulation and design-based claims, and identifies the narrow nulls for which finite permutation validity is available.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: cluster trials use pairing to improve balance -> conventional regression inference can be conservative and ATE theory is largely asymptotic -> exact prediction of a selected cluster's causal effect requires a pair-swap-specific conformal validity rule

method_exemplars.technique: Represent assignment as independent within-pair label swaps and derive the exact randomization distribution conditional on the matched clusters.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The requested S2 reduction uses exactly the within-pair randomization law formalized in this paper.

method_exemplars.technique: Form model-robust conformal scores for cluster-level causal effects and invert their finite-sample ranks into prediction intervals.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies the causal-effect score and inversion machinery that must be specialized to pair swaps.

method_exemplars.technique: Use design-based pair-respecting weighting and randomization variance calculations, with proofs showing why analyses that ignore the pair structure fail.
method_exemplars.why_closest: Mate leakage is another failure caused by using information in a way that does not respect the matched-pair assignment mechanism.

literature_map:
Wang, Li, and Yu (2025, Statistica Sinica preprint SS-2025-0476) is the direct anchor: finite-sample conformal prediction of cluster- and individual-level treatment effects with arbitrary working outcome models under its stated randomization regime.
Lei and Candès (2021, JRSSB, arXiv:2006.06138) established finite-sample average coverage for counterfactual and individual-treatment-effect intervals in completely or stratified randomized experiments.
Barber, Candès, Ramdas, and Tibshirani (2023, Annals of Statistics, arXiv:2202.13415) quantify robustness beyond exchangeability but do not provide sharp finite-orbit necessity results.
Jin and Ren (2025, JRSSB, arXiv:2403.03868) show that focal-unit selection breaks exchangeability and restore exact selection-conditional coverage through replay-based reference sets.
Dunn, Wasserman, and Ramdas (2023, JASA, arXiv:1809.07441) address two-layer hierarchical nonexchangeability via pooled and subsampling conformal constructions.
Hemerik and Goeman (2018, TEST, DOI:10.1007/s11749-017-0571-1) provide the foundational finite-group and orbit proof for exact tests with random permutations.
Ramdas, Barber, Candès, and Tibshirani (2023, Sankhya A, arXiv:2204.13581) show that valid randomized permutation tests can use arbitrary distributions over arbitrary permutation subsets.
Koning and Hemerik (2024, Biometrika, arXiv:2202.00967) preserve exactness with strategically selected subgroups and expose computational and leakage tradeoffs.
Bai, Liu, Shaikh, and Tabord-Meehan (2024, Journal of Econometrics, arXiv:2211.14903) derive matched-pair cluster inference, with finite randomization validity confined to specific null hypotheses and ATE results mainly asymptotic.
Imai, King, and Nall (2009, Statistical Science, arXiv:0910.3752) show why pair-matched cluster analyses must respect the design and give design-based estimators for small samples.
Small, Ten Have, and Rosenbaum (2012, Biometrics, DOI:10.1111/j.1541-0420.2011.01622.x) develop exact rank-based randomization inference for matched pairs of randomized groups.
Wieczorek (2023, Survey Methodology, arXiv:2303.01422) transplants conformal prediction into finite-population design-based inference and calls for work on complex sampling designs.
Gibbs, Cherian, and Candès (2023, arXiv:2305.12616) obtain exact finite-sample guarantees over finite-dimensional classes of covariate shifts, clarifying the boundary between feasible restricted conditioning and impossible universal conditional coverage.
Cohen et al. (2022, JAMA Network Open, DOI:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2022.46158) supply the Shamba Maisha consumer: 16 Kenyan health facilities randomized as eight matched pairs.
The central tension is that exact conformal CRT methods provide sufficient constructions, while group-invariance, arbitrary-support, and selective-conformal papers expose ingredients for but do not state a sound-and-complete orbit-balance certificate with sharp failure bounds.
prior_proposal_map:
The closest prior qid was stat_conformal_overlap_frontier/v1, mined from its bank README and all five angle1 reviewer JSONs.
Its highest-yield strengthening_paths prescribed a finite certificate representation, coherent candidate-specific target sampling, and a nondegenerate shell-richness lower construction.
The angle1_v4 reviewer identified the decisive coupling defect: one target draw cannot simultaneously have several incompatible candidate marginals.
That same review prescribed conditioning on the actual selector or ratio before claiming uniform validity over laws.
The bank summary says the learned-weight frontier was not supportable and identifies list-learning or identification structure, or a new validity criterion, as the re-raise route.
Rejected angle0, angle2, and angle3 proposal files were inspected but contained unfilled templates and yielded no substantive gap excerpts.
The accepted exp_design_pm1_exactness_boundary_v1 and downgraded exp_ego_cluster_exposure_clt banks were also inspected; their open design and variance gaps were too remote from the present selector-orbit kernel to retain.
No earlier proposal, reviewer, or bank README mentions Shamba Maisha, the eight-pair consumer, orbit-balanced calibration and target selectors, or paired-mate leakage.
